title: Bayes' Theorem
definition: A mathematical formula for updating the probability of a hypothesis based on new evidence. It relates conditional and marginal probabilities, providing a framework for probabilistic inference and belief revision.

proofs.description: Derived from definition of conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B)
proofs.reference: Bayes, T. (1763). An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances. Philosophical Transactions, 53, 370-418.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Bayes' Theorem helps you update your beliefs when you get new information. It tells you how much to change your mind based on evidence.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Bayes' Theorem quantifies belief revision: posterior probability equals likelihood times prior divided by evidence. It provides a normative framework for learning from data.
difficulty_levels.expert: Bayesian inference uses Bayes' Theorem to update probability distributions. It generalizes to continuous cases, forms the basis for Bayesian statistics (credible intervals, Bayes factors), and connects to information theory and Occam's razor (marginal likelihood penalizes complexity).

extra_fields.historical_context: Published posthumously by Thomas Bayes (1763). Independently rediscovered by Laplace (1774). Modern Bayesian revolution began mid-20th century (Jeffreys, Savage, Jaynes).
extra_fields.limitations: Requires specifying prior probabilities, which can be subjective. Computationally intensive for complex models.
